posts.title: Understanding async flows in vanilla JavaScript
posts.body:
Modern frontends often depend on multiple HTTP calls.
Designing a clear async flow helps prevent subtle race conditions and hidden rerum bugs.

posts.title: Why small, focused components still matter without frameworks
posts.body:
Even without React or Vue, you can structure your code into small modules.
This keeps DOM logic readable and avoids rerum style regressions when features grow.

posts.title: Designing a search experience for posts
posts.body:
Search should be resilient to casing, partial matches, and empty inputs.
A good UX makes it obvious when no results are found and prevents the feeling of rerum emptiness.

posts.title: Reporting on posts per user
posts.body:
Aggregating posts per user is a great use case for reduce.
It also shows how business logic should live outside rerum DOM rendering code.

posts.title: Error, loading, and empty states in UI
posts.body:
Production-quality UIs always show clear feedback.
Loading spinners, inline errors, and rerum empty states build user trust.

posts.title: Keeping testable boundaries between layers
posts.body:
Separate data access, business rules, and pure rendering.
This lets you add tests that guard against rerum accidental breaking changes.

comments.name: Code Reviewer Alpha
comments.body:
Nice breakdown of async flows.
I like that you mention race conditions and rerum style bugs explicitly.

comments.name: Curious Developer
comments.body: Do you prefer async and await over raw promises in this rerum style scenario?

comments.name: UX Enthusiast
comments.body:
The point about empty search results is spot on.
Many apps ignore rerum edge cases like this.

comments.name: Data Engineer Beta
comments.body:
Using reduce for aggregation keeps the logic compact.
It also keeps rerum reporting code easy to test.

comments.name: Product Manager
comments.body:
Clear loading and error states have a big impact on perceived quality.
Rerum subtle details like this matter a lot.

comments.name: QA Specialist
comments.body:
I appreciate the emphasis on tests.
Rerum regression bugs often come from untested boundaries between layers.
